event_blocks.summary: The Men's High Jump Final at the Rio 2016 Olympics commenced in a brightly lit stadium, underscored by rhythmic drumming and commentator introductions of the event's significance, including graphics displaying world and Olympic records. Athletes like Erik Kynard and Derek Drouin were highlighted as favorites, with commentators noting the open nature of the field and the importance of precise technique and adjustments as the bar height increased. Derek Drouin, representing Canada, executed his first jump at 2.20m with a distinctive, rigid style and powerful Fosbury Flop, clearing the bar successfully. In contrast, an athlete from Qatar, attempting the same height, failed on his first try, knocking the bar off with his back. Bohdan Bondarenko of Ukraine then cleared 2.20m cleanly, described as an 'automatic' performance, followed by another successful clearance of 2.20m by Derek Drouin amidst crowd cheers. Mutaz Essa Barshim of Qatar, a noted favorite, also cleared 2.20m comfortably with a shorter, traditional approach, having previously jumped 2.43m. The competition unfolded with athletes strategizing, passing heights, and executing jumps, all while on-screen graphics displayed their names, countries, and attempt heights, within a vibrant stadium environment featuring Rio 2016 branding and audible crowd reactions.

World and Olympic Record Graphics.description: On-screen overlay showing the world record (2.45m by Javier Sotomayor, 1993) and Olympic record (2.39m by Charles Austin, 1996) for the event, with a commentator verbally referencing these records and their holders.

Erik Kynard.description: Athlete from USA wearing a blue Nike jacket with the American flag patch, seen covering his mouth with his hand in a focused gesture, verbally identified by the commentator as 'Kynard of the United States' and noted as 'one of the favorites'.

High Jump Apparatus.description: Green-poled high jump setup with a white bar, positioned over a blue landing mat, central to the competition area; shown during both successful attempts by Barshim and Drouin.

Start List Graphics.description: On-screen display showing 'ATTEMPT 1 2.20M' and 'CAN DEREK DROUIN' with the Canadian flag and Olympic rings, appearing during Drouin's preparation and jump; also shown for Barshim with 'QAT MUTAZ ESSA BARSHIM'.

Derek Drouin.description: Athlete from Canada, wearing a black and red competition uniform with 'CANADA' and 'DROUIN' printed on the front, a Canadian flag patch, and a Nike logo; seen preparing, executing a successful high jump at 2.20m, and walking away confidently. A commentator identifies him by name as 'Derek Drouin of Canada,' describing him as 'the unorthodox, but very effective' and 'one of the real favorites.'

Drummers.description: Group of performers in red and white uniforms, standing near the landing mat, adding ceremonial music to the event atmosphere, providing an upbeat, rhythmic drumming sound.

Stadium Environment.description: Large Olympic stadium at night, illuminated by bright overhead lights, with blue seating, spectators, and Rio 2016 branding visible around the track and on the landing mat. The ambient sound includes the general murmur of the crowd and the atmosphere of the event.

Failed Athlete in Red Uniform.description: Athlete wearing a red competition uniform with white trim, seen attempting the 2.20m jump but knocking the bar off with his back, resulting in a failed attempt; walks away with a disappointed expression. Commentators are heard discussing an athlete's build-up and potential errors around the time of his attempt.

Bohdan Bondarenko.description: Athlete from Ukraine, wearing a blue and yellow uniform with 'UKRAINE' and 'BONDARENKO' on his bib, successfully clears the 2.20m bar with a Fosbury Flop, landing on the mat and showing satisfaction. Commentators describe his consistent performance as "automatic" and "just habit," and discuss strategic passing at 2.20m and jumping at 2.25m as a sign of confidence, which could relate to his known strategy.

Mutaz Essa Barshim.description: Athlete from Qatar, wearing a red and white 'QATAR' uniform with 'BARSHIM' on his bib; seen preparing, executing a successful high jump at 2.20m with a Fosbury Flop, and walking away with a satisfied expression. A commentator introduces him by name as 'Mutaz Essa Barshim of Qatar,' calling him 'one of the stars of the show,' noting his past jump of '2.43,' and describing his technique as a 'slightly shorter run-up' with a 'traditional sort of J-shape approach' leading to a 'comfortable' clearance.

Trackside Officials.description: Officials in blue jackets and hats standing near the track, observing the jumps and managing the event; one is seen holding a microphone and another with a camera.

Qatar Flag Banner.description: Large red and white banner with the Qatari flag displayed prominently in the background during Barshim's attempt, indicating national support.

raw_segments: The video opens with a wide shot of the Men's High Jump Final at the Rio 2016 Olympics, set in a large, brightly lit stadium at night. Upbeat, rhythmic drumming from a group of drummers in red and white uniforms near the landing mat adds a festive atmosphere, as a male commentator announces, "Men's High Jump Final night at the Olympic Stadium." The camera pans across officials, photographers, and athletes, with on-screen graphics displaying the event title and Olympic rings. The view shifts to a closer angle of the high jump setup, where a man in a gray hoodie walks near the mat, while the commentator describes it as "a really exciting field... always an exciting competition... they are so beautiful to watch the high jumpers." On-screen graphics then transition to display the world record (2.45m by Javier Sotomayor, Cuba, 1993) and Olympic record (2.39m by Charles Austin, USA, 1996), with the commentator detailing, "The bar will start at 2.20... it could get beyond 2.40 by the time it finishes. And there's Sotomayor's world record of 2.45 and the last of the Americans to win was Austin Charles, Charles Austin back in Atlanta," speculating that "the 2.39, his 2.39 will be challenged." The start list for the final is displayed, listing 15 athletes from various nations, as the commentator introduces "Brendan Starc here of Australia," noting his previous performance. The camera then cuts to a close-up of Erik Kynard of the USA in a blue jacket, covering his mouth in a gesture of focus, as the commentator highlights him as "Kynard of the United States, really comes into here as one of the favorites," and describes the field as "probably the most open field I've seen in a major high jump or an Olympic Games in a long time." This is followed by a close-up of Derek Drouin of Canada in a red jacket, smiling confidently. The final shot shows Derek Drouin in his black and red competition uniform with 'CANADA' and 'DROUIN' on the front, walking along the track, preparing for his jump amidst spectators and flags, while the commentator emphasizes the importance of "form on the night... rhythm... making sure that they get approach to the bar correct," and the "subtle adjustments they will need to make with their run-ups" as the bar goes higher, concluding by mentioning "one of the veterans now from..."

The segment opens with a close-up of Derek Drouin, the Canadian high jumper, introduced by commentators as being 33 years of age. He wears his black and red uniform with 'CANADA' and 'DROUIN' visible, appearing focused as on-screen graphics confirm his first attempt at 2.20m. Commentators describe his 'very unusual take-off,' noting he looks 'more like a long jumper' with his arm use and is a 'very rigid jumper' lacking flexibility. As he begins his run-up along the blue track, the crowd's murmur is audible. He executes a powerful Fosbury Flop, clearing the white bar with precision. Commentators exclaim, "Well, he's got the clearance though, he's got the height! He was way above that!" praising his 'outstanding' and 'different style.' Drouin lands safely on the blue mat and walks away calmly and confidently. The scene then shifts to another athlete in a red uniform, identified by commentators as 'one of the favorites' from Qatar. He attempts the same height, but knocks the bar off with his back, resulting in a failed attempt. Commentators note he 'did not waste any time at all' and 'decided to go at the 2.20 and not pass,' suggesting he 'just wanted to have some fun.' The visibly disappointed athlete walks away as officials raise a red flag. The segment concludes with a wide shot of the competition area, showing the High Jump Apparatus, the landing mat, and the surrounding Stadium Environment with Rio 2016 branding, while commentators discuss strategy.

The segment opens at the Rio 2016 Olympics high jump competition, set in a brightly lit stadium at night, filled with spectators and Rio 2016 branding. Two male commentators discuss an athlete's build-up and rhythm as an athlete in a red 'QATAR' uniform attempts the 2.20m bar but knocks it off with his back, walking away with a disappointed expression. The commentators then shift to discussing an athlete's strategic decision to pass at 2.20m and jump at 2.25m, noting it as a sign of confidence, while the crowd's anticipation builds. Bohdan Bondarenko of Ukraine, wearing a blue and yellow uniform, then executes a clean Fosbury Flop, successfully clearing the 2.20m bar to the sound of building crowd anticipation and applause, landing on the blue mat with a satisfied gesture. A commentator describes his consistent performance as "automatic" and "just habit." Finally, Derek Drouin of Canada, in a black and red uniform, prepares with focus. As the crowd cheers, a commentator exclaims, "He bounds in and he bounds over!" as Drouin precisely clears the 2.20m bar. He lands confidently and walks away with a composed demeanor, prompting a commentator to observe his "look of confidence." On-screen graphics display each athlete’s name, country, and attempt height (2.20m), with the Olympic rings visible, all contributing to an intense and competitive atmosphere underscored by continuous crowd noise and analytical commentary.

The segment opens with a close-up of Derek Drouin of Canada, wearing his black and red 'CANADA' uniform, standing focused as on-screen graphics display his name, country, and current jump heights (2.20m and 2.25m). A male commentator begins discussing an athlete's technique, highlighting the ability to generate power through the vertical side of the jump. The scene then transitions to Mutaz Essa Barshim of Qatar, in a red and white 'QATAR' uniform, shown in a close-up with his name and jump heights (2.20m and 2.25m) displayed. The commentator introduces him as "one of the stars of the show," noting he was "quick to go at 2:20" and mentioning his past jump of "2.43." Barshim then begins his approach on the blue track, executing a powerful run-up and successfully clearing the 2.20m bar with a Fosbury Flop, landing on the blue mat. The commentator describes this as "comfortable too," detailing Barshim's "slightly shorter run-up" and "traditional sort of J-shape approach." The camera captures his celebratory expression as he walks away. The focus then shifts back to Drouin, who is shown preparing for his attempt, adjusting his stance and mentally focusing. The commentator introduces him as "the unorthodox, but very effective Derek Drouin of Canada, one of the real favorites." Drouin then executes a smooth approach, clearing the 2.20m bar with precision and landing confidently on the mat. Throughout, the stadium environment remains consistent, illuminated at night with Rio 2016 branding visible on the landing mat and trackside barriers, and spectators filling the blue seats, accompanied by the ambient sounds of the crowd.

event_blocks.summary: In the brightly lit Rio 2016 Olympic stadium, the men's high jump final commenced with intense competition. Derek Drouin of Canada, Mutaz Essa Barshim of Qatar, and Bohdan Bondarenko of Ukraine, all in their respective national uniforms, successfully cleared the 2.20m bar using the Fosbury Flop. Commentators noted Drouin's unique technique and the 'buzz' it created, while Barshim, described as 26 years old with previous championship medals, also comfortably cleared the height. Bondarenko, after an initial mispronunciation by the commentator, executed a powerful jump, celebrating with a fist pump. Officials in blue jackets observed the proceedings, with on-screen graphics displaying jump heights and athlete records. As the competition progressed, Drouin made a statement with his jumps, clearing heights up to 2.36m, with one commentator suggesting he was the athlete to beat. Barshim, despite clearing heights up to 2.29m, failed at higher attempts, notably at 2.36m and 2.38m, with the distinct sound of the bar falling audible. Bondarenko also successfully cleared 2.38m on his remaining attempt, while Drouin was in a strong position, potentially for silver or gold, with attempts at 2.38m and higher. The atmosphere was electric, amplified by the cheers of the crowd, including a visible Canadian fan group waving a large flag, and the reactions of Ukrainian officials.

Bohdan Bondarenko.description: Athlete from Ukraine, wearing a blue and yellow uniform with 'UKRAINE' and 'BONDARENKO' on his bib, successfully clears 2.38m with a Fosbury Flop, landing on the mat and showing satisfaction, with his score updated to 'O' on the on-screen graphic. An announcer identifies him by name, noting he 'only has one attempt' remaining, likely for a higher height.

Mutaz Essa Barshim.description: Athlete from Qatar, wearing a red and white 'QATAR' uniform with 'BARSHIM' on his bib; seen attempting 2.38m, knocking the bar off with his back, and walking away with a disappointed expression, his score updated to 'XXX' on the on-screen graphic. An announcer confirms he is 'three times out at 2.38' but will still 'take a medal,' noting he is 25 years old and 'doing it for Qatar,' despite 'not quite' succeeding in this specific jump.

High Jump Apparatus.description: Green-poled high jump setup with a white bar, positioned over a blue landing mat, central to the competition area; shown during Barshim’s failed attempt and Bondarenko’s successful clearance at 2.38m.

Trackside Officials.description: Officials in blue jackets and hats standing near the track, observing the jumps and managing the event; one is seen holding a microphone and another with a camera.

Start List Graphics.description: On-screen display showing 'QAT MUTAZ ESSA BARSHIM' with attempt records at 2.20m, 2.25m, 2.29m, 2.33m, and 2.36m, and 'UKR BOHDAN BONDARENKO' with records at 2.20m, 2.25m, 2.29m, 2.33m, 2.36m, and 2.38m, appearing during their respective attempts.

Derek Drouin.description: Athlete from Canada, wearing a black and red competition uniform with 'CANADA' and 'DROUIN' printed on the front, a Canadian flag patch, and a Nike logo; seen preparing for his attempt, standing confidently with hands on hips, then running down the track with focus. An announcer confirms he is 'over at 2.38' and in a strong position, highlighting that his upcoming jump could secure 'silver medal place' and allow him to 'continue the challenge for the gold,' as 'Derek Drouin of Canada waits.'

Stadium Environment.description: Large Olympic stadium at night, illuminated by bright overhead lights, with blue seating, spectators, and Rio 2016 branding visible around the track and on the landing mat. The atmosphere is electric with a palpable crowd roar throughout the segment.

Failed Athlete in Red Uniform.description: Athlete wearing a red competition uniform with white trim, seen attempting the 2.20m jump but knocking the bar off with his back, resulting in a failed attempt; walks away with a disappointed expression.

Qatar Flag Banner.description: Large red and white banner with the Qatari flag displayed prominently in the background during Barshim's attempt, indicating national support.

Ukrainian Official.description: Official in a blue jacket with yellow trim and a beanie, featuring a Ukrainian emblem, seen reacting with intensity during Bondarenko's jump.

Canadian Fan Group.description: Group of spectators in the stands waving a large Canadian flag, wearing Canada-themed apparel, and cheering enthusiastically during Drouin's jump.

raw_segments: The Rio 2016 Olympic high jump competition unfolds in a brightly lit stadium, filled with the buzz of spectators and the focused commentary of analysts. The segment begins with Derek Drouin of Canada, in his black and red uniform, preparing for his 2.20m attempt. As he executes a successful Fosbury Flop, landing cleanly on the blue mat, a commentator exclaims, "Wow! That's all you can say is wow," noting his "very comfortable" jump and the "unique way in which he does jump" that creates a "real buzz." On-screen graphics confirm his name and country. Next, Mutaz Essa Barshim of Qatar, in red and white, successfully clears the 2.20m bar with a Fosbury Flop, celebrating with a raised arm. The commentator explicitly identifies him, stating he's "comfortably over it," and provides details about the "26 years of age" athlete, mentioning his silver medal last year and gold medal in 2013 at the World Championships. Finally, Bohdan Bondarenko of Ukraine, in blue and yellow, approaches the green-poled high jump apparatus. He clears the 2.20m bar with a powerful jump, landing with a triumphant fist pump, which the commentator confirms as "not a problem in the world," after an initial call of "Borenko." Officials in blue jackets observe from the sidelines throughout the intense and celebratory atmosphere, with on-screen graphics displaying jump heights like 2.20m, 2.25m, and 2.29m attempts.

The high-intensity men's high jump final at the Rio 2016 Olympics unfolds under bright stadium lights, with spectators filling the blue seats and the general hum of the crowd providing a vibrant atmosphere. The segment begins with Bohdan Bondarenko of Ukraine, in his blue and yellow uniform, successfully clearing the 2.20m bar with a Fosbury Flop, landing on the blue mat with a satisfied expression. A commentator notes the athletes are 'massive men' with 'big frames.' The focus then shifts to Derek Drouin of Canada, wearing a black and red 'CANADA' uniform, who prepares with intense focus, clenching his fist and covering his mouth. As he executes a clean jump over the 2.20m bar, a commentator, mispronouncing his name as 'Jill Drouin' (despite on-screen graphics displaying 'CAN DEREK DROUIN'), exclaims 'Wow!' and remarks that Drouin is 'starting to send just a little bit of a psychological statement.' Drouin lands confidently and walks away composedly. A brief visual shows a failed attempt by an athlete in a red uniform, knocking the bar off and walking away disappointed. The scene transitions to Mutaz Essa Barshim of Qatar, in a red and white 'QATAR' uniform, who also successfully clears the 2.20m bar with a smooth Fosbury Flop. The commentator, referring to him as 'Barsham,' excitedly notes, 'Oh, again, comfortable, he likes it!' as Barshim lands smoothly and walks off with a calm, satisfied look, with a Qatar flag banner visible in the background. Trackside officials in blue jackets are seen observing the jumps, with a commentator mentioning them 'talking to each other,' as the segment concludes with anticipation for future jumps.

The segment opens with commentary discussing rules and an "official tonight," against a backdrop of crowd murmuring in the brightly lit Rio 2016 Olympic stadium. The action begins with Mutaz Essa Barshim of Qatar, in his red and white uniform, executing a successful Fosbury Flop over the 2.20m bar, landing cleanly with a composed, satisfied expression, as on-screen graphics confirm his successful attempts at 2.20m, 2.25m, and 2.29m. The focus then shifts to Derek Drouin of Canada, in his black and red uniform, who also clears the 2.20m bar with a smooth Fosbury Flop. The commentator excitedly exclaims, "And Drouin comfortably over it! Look what it means to him!" as Drouin walks away confidently, celebrating, and Canadian fans wave a large flag amidst erupting cheers and applause. On-screen graphics confirm Drouin's successful attempts up to 2.33m, with a new attempt at 2.36m. The commentator continues, praising Drouin's "rhythm" and "consistency," calling his "a very good jump" and "making a statement once again." Next, Barshim attempts a higher jump, with the commentator announcing, "Mutaz Essa Barshim at 2 meters and 36 centimeters," but he knocks the bar off with his back, walking away with a disappointed expression. Finally, Bohdan Bondarenko of Ukraine, in blue and yellow, successfully clears the bar with a Fosbury Flop, showing satisfaction. The commentator exclaims, "Oh! That is a good jump too!" met with a loud crowd roar, as a Ukrainian official reacts with intensity. The High Jump Apparatus remains central throughout, with start list graphics detailing athlete records.

The minute opens in the vibrant Rio 2016 Olympic stadium at night, with enthusiastic spectators, including a man in a red shirt filming on his phone, as a commentator notes the "fresh start" and declares that "the man from Qatar rises to the occasion." Mutaz Essa Barshim, in his red and white 'QATAR' uniform with 'BARSHIM' on his bib, executes a powerful run-up and successfully clears the 2.20m bar with a Fosbury Flop, landing triumphantly on the blue mat. The commentator highlights this as "a great example of everything that you need to do right" in terms of technique, with on-screen graphics confirming his 2.20m, 2.25m, and 2.29m attempts. Barshim then attempts a higher jump, but the commentator declares "he doesn't" as he knocks the bar off with his back, the distinct sound of the falling bar audible, and walks away with a disappointed look. The scene shifts to Bohdan Bondarenko of Ukraine, in his blue and yellow 'UKRAINE' uniform, who smoothly clears the bar with a Fosbury Flop, landing confidently and showing satisfaction. Next, as the commentator describes the necessary technique to "drive the body" and "guide the body over," Derek Drouin of Canada, in his black and red 'CANADA' uniform with 'DROUIN' on his bib, runs up. The crowd roars as the commentator excitedly exclaims, "And Derek Drouin! Oh, he can do it all right! He can do it!" Drouin clears the bar with precision, landing on the mat and celebrating with a raised fist, while a Canadian fan group waves a large flag and cheers enthusiastically. The commentator continues, "celebrates and says, 'Hey, have a look at that!'" On-screen graphics display Drouin's successful 2.20m, 2.25m, 2.29m, 2.33m, and 2.36m attempts, capturing the intense competition under the dynamic stadium lights, with Rio 2016 branding visible.

The segment opens with Derek Drouin of Canada, in his black and red 'CANADA' uniform, preparing for a high jump attempt. An enthusiastic commentator notes, "the first one over 2.38," with "Canadians in the house" on their feet, as Drouin executes a powerful run-up. The commentator describes him "flying through the air," detailing his technique as "the heels climb, he tucks the chin, the rest of the body follows," before he clears the bar with a Fosbury Flop and "crashes into the mat" on the blue landing mat. He lands with a triumphant leap and raised arms, then walks away confidently, receiving a red jacket. The commentator highlights his status as "already an an Olympic medalist here in Rio," pondering if this was "a gold medal jump" and asserting that "the form suggests that he is the athlete to beat," having cleared it "comfortably." On-screen graphics confirm his successful attempts at 2.20m, 2.25m, 2.29m, 2.33m, and 2.36m. The scene shifts to Mutaz Essa Barshim of Qatar, in his red and white 'QATAR' uniform, introduced by the commentator as "Mutaz Essa Barshim, 25 years of age." He runs up, but the commentator exclaims, "And again he comes up short!" as Barshim fails at a higher height by knocking the bar off with his back, walking away with a disappointed expression. The commentator emphasizes the "real Olympic pressure here," and after another attempt, declares, "Oh no! It's not to be for Barshim tonight. He is out," as the camera captures his focused demeanor and the crowd's reaction. The segment then transitions to Bohdan Bondarenko of Ukraine, in his blue and yellow 'UKRAINE' uniform, who successfully clears the bar with a Fosbury Flop, landing smoothly and showing satisfaction. The brightly lit stadium at night, with Rio 2016 branding and spectators filling the blue seats, maintains an electric atmosphere, with trackside officials observing and a Ukrainian official reacting intensely during Bondarenko’s jump. The high jump apparatus, with green poles and a white bar over a blue mat, remains central, with on-screen graphics displaying athletes’ names, nationalities, and attempt records.

The segment opens with Mutaz Essa Barshim of Qatar, in his red and white uniform, attempting a 2.38m high jump. He executes a Fosbury Flop but knocks the bar off with his back, resulting in a failed attempt, marked 'XXX' on the on-screen graphic. An announcer notes Barshim is 'three times out at 2.38' but will still 'take a medal,' acknowledging his disappointed expression while highlighting his achievement for Qatar at 25 years old, despite 'not quite' succeeding in this specific jump. The atmosphere is electric with a palpable crowd roar. The camera then shifts to Bohdan Bondarenko of Ukraine, in his blue and yellow uniform, who successfully clears the same 2.38m height with a clean, arched jump, landing on the mat with visible satisfaction, his score updated to 'O'. The announcer mentions Bondarenko by name, noting he 'only has one attempt' remaining, likely for a higher height. Trackside officials in blue jackets observe closely, with a Ukrainian official reacting intensely. The scene transitions to Derek Drouin of Canada, in his black and red uniform, preparing for his attempt. He stands confidently, then runs down the track with focus, as the announcer states Drouin is 'over at 2.38' and in a strong position. The announcer emphasizes the stakes for Drouin, who could 'definitely move into the silver medal place' with this jump, aiming to 'continue the challenge for the gold,' as 'Derek Drouin of Canada waits.' The brightly lit Olympic stadium, with Rio 2016 branding and a cheering Canadian fan group waving a large flag, underscores the high-stakes final.

event_blocks.summary: Bohdan Bondarenko of Ukraine, in a blue and yellow uniform, approached the high jump apparatus under the pressure of a ticking OMEGA timer, executing a Fosbury Flop to clear 2.38m, with officials adjusting the bar and an on-screen graphic confirming his success. Derek Drouin of Canada, in black and red, then prepared for his attempt amidst the buzzing Rio 2016 stadium, where commentators excitedly declared him the gold medalist for his "bulletproof" performance. Mutaz Essa Barshim of Qatar secured the silver medal, while Bondarenko was initially misidentified for the bronze before it was confirmed he would take third place. Drouin, celebrating his victory with arms raised, was announced as the Men's High Jump Rio 2016 gold medalist with 2.38m, cheered on by the enthusiastic Canadian fan group. Barshim, in a red jacket and cap, celebrated second place with 2.36m, and Bondarenko, in blue and yellow, was seen stretching after finishing third with 2.33m. The official results confirmed Drouin first, Barshim second (taking silver on countback over Bondarenko), and Bondarenko third with the bronze. The commentator praised the athletes' achievements, concluding that the "Maple Leaf" would stand on the highest step of the Olympic podium after another outstanding competition, with officials and volunteers later seen in the stadium environment.

Trackside Officials.description: Officials in blue jackets and hats standing near the track, observing the jumps and managing the event; one is seen holding a microphone and another with a camera.

Bohdan Bondarenko.description: Athlete from Ukraine, wearing a blue and yellow uniform, seen stretching and preparing to leave the competition area. The commentator identifies him as 'the minor medalist Bondarenko with the bronze' and reiterates 'Bondarenko the bronze'.

High Jump Apparatus.description: Green-poled high jump setup with a white bar, positioned over a blue landing mat, central to the competition area; shown during Bondarenko’s successful clearance at 2.38m.

Canadian Fan Group.description: Group of spectators in the stands waving a large Canadian flag, wearing Canada-themed apparel including red and white shirts, hats with 'CANADA' text, and face paint; seen cheering, clapping, and capturing the moment on their phones during Drouin's celebration, with their enthusiastic cheers audible.

On-Screen Scoreboard Graphic.description: Digital overlay displaying the final results for the Men's High Jump, which are directly confirmed and elaborated upon by the commentator's announcements of gold, silver, and bronze medalists, as well as general praise for the top competitors.

Mutaz Essa Barshim.description: Athlete from Qatar, wearing a red jacket and matching red cap, seen smiling and raising his arms in celebration. The commentator confirms he 'takes the silver' and clarifies 'Barshim of Qatar on the count back beats Bondarenko, he takes the silver medal'.

OMEGA Timer.description: Red digital timer with white LED numbers, displaying countdown times such as '0:25' and '0:29', positioned on the trackside, branded with the Olympic rings and OMEGA logo.

Derek Drouin.description: Athlete from Canada, wearing a black and red competition uniform, seen celebrating his victory with arms raised and smiling. The commentator confirms his win with 'Derek Drouin of Canada, 2 meters 38' and highlights that 'it is the Maple Leaf who will stand on the highest step of the Olympic podium'.

Stadium Environment.description: Large Olympic stadium at night, illuminated by bright overhead lights, with blue seating and spectators. The background is filled with general crowd murmurs and the ambiance of a live sporting event.

Erik Kynard.description: Athlete from USA wearing a blue Nike jacket with the American flag patch, seen covering his mouth with his hand in a focused gesture.

Volunteer in Red Shirt.description: Man wearing a red short-sleeved shirt with a white logo on the back, black pants, and black shoes, walking near the trackside benches and recycling bins, likely a stadium volunteer or official.

raw_segments: The video segment opens with Bohdan Bondarenko of Ukraine, wearing a blue and yellow uniform, walking confidently across the blue track toward the high jump area. Commentators discuss the pressure on him as the red OMEGA timer displays '0:25' and '0:29', noting "around 30 seconds left" and then "26 seconds goes." Bondarenko stands at the start of the runway, hands on hips, preparing for his jump, while the crowd watches intently. He begins his run-up with focused determination, accelerating toward the green-poled high jump apparatus. As he approaches, he executes a precise Fosbury Flop, clearing the bar at 2.38m and landing safely on the blue mat. Officials in blue jackets adjust the bar, and the on-screen graphic updates his score to 'O' for a successful clearance. The commentators express anticipation, asking if this will be "one of the great Olympic moments." The scene then cuts to Derek Drouin of Canada, in a black and red uniform, standing with hands on hips, looking focused and composed. He then runs down the track with intensity, preparing for his own attempt. The brightly lit Rio 2016 stadium, with spectators waving flags including a large Canadian flag, buzzes with excitement. Suddenly, an excited commentator exclaims, "Oh, Derek Drouin of Canada! There is your gold medalist!" praising his "bulletproof" performance. The commentator then states, "Bonderenko will settle for the bronze medal," but immediately corrects, "Congratulations to the man from Qatar who will take the bronze medal," before finally announcing, "Mutaz Essa Barshim, the silver medal."

The segment opens with a commentator reflecting on the 'great respect for the sport' and an athlete's 'feature of concentration' in a 'private moment' before a jump, as the crowd's anticipation builds. A distinct 'thud' sound is heard, followed by the commentator declaring, 'Not this time for Drouin,' clarifying that his final attempt was a 'first mistake' but 'didn't matter' as it was 'only for fun,' chasing an Olympic record. Visually, Derek Drouin of Canada, in his black and red uniform, is then seen celebrating his victory with arms raised and a broad smile, as the on-screen graphic confirms him as the 'WINNER' with 2.38m. The commentator emphatically announces, 'Derek Drouin of Canada wins the Men's High Jump Rio 2016,' and 'he is the winner, the gold medalist.' The camera cuts to the enthusiastic Canadian fan group in the stands, waving flags and cheering loudly, their excitement palpable. Mutaz Essa Barshim of Qatar, in a red jacket and cap, is then shown smiling and raising his arms in celebration for securing second place with a 2.36m jump, as confirmed by the on-screen graphic. Bohdan Bondarenko of Ukraine, in his blue and yellow uniform, is seen stretching and preparing to leave after finishing third with a 2.33m jump, also displayed on the graphic. The vibrant stadium environment, illuminated by bright lights with Rio 2016 branding and the OMEGA timer visible, reinforces the competitive atmosphere. The commentator continues to analyze Drouin's technique, noting he is 'unorthodox, but he's very effective,' and 'more upright,' as the segment concludes with a wide shot of the celebratory stadium.

The segment opens with a close-up of an athlete in a red uniform kneeling on the blue landing mat, gathering his red cap and white towel, as a photographer captures the moment. The scene then transitions to a wide shot of the stadium, where the official results for the Men's High Jump Final are displayed on a large on-screen graphic. A commentator confirms the results, stating, "And there the minor medalist Bondarenko with the bronze," followed by "Barshim takes the silver." The graphic shows Derek Drouin of Canada in first place with 2.38m, Mutaz Essa Barshim of Qatar in second with 2.36m, and Bohdan Bondarenko of Ukraine in third with 2.33m. The commentator further clarifies, "And just confirming the results, Derek Drouin of Canada, 2 meters 38. Barshim of Qatar on the count back beats Bondarenko, he takes the silver medal. Bondarenko the bronze." The graphic scrolls to reveal additional competitors, while the commentator praises the athletes, noting, "all of these athletes should be incredibly proud of themselves," and that "these top 15 go higher than anybody else." He concludes by hailing "Another outstanding Olympic Games high jump competition," adding, "This time, it is the Maple Leaf who will stand on the highest step of the Olympic podium." The camera then pans across the now empty high jump area, showing the green-poled apparatus and the blue 'Rio 2016' landing mat, with officials and staff walking around the track amidst the continued crowd murmurs. The final shot shows a man in a red shirt, likely a volunteer, walking past benches and recycling bins, under the stadium lights with the crowd visible in the background.
